data.title: Lululemon's stock is still a buy, even as customers are feeling the squeeze
data.description:
By James Rogers

Lululemon shares are tumbling after the company gave weaker-than-expected guidance when it reported fourth-quarter results

Lululemon Athletica Inc. may be wrestling with tariffs, consumer uncertainty and a pressing need to boost brand awareness, but analysts still see plenty of positives ahead for the athleisure giant.

The company (LULU) reported fourth-quarter results late Thursday, with weaker-than-expected guidance amid "ongoing macro uncertainties," sending its stock tumbling.

The stock was last down 14.6%, putting it on track for its biggest one-day percentage decline in about a year. The last time it fell this much was March 22, 2024, when it closed down 15.8%, according to Dow Jones Market Data.

While the outlook embeds low-single-digit revenue growth in the U.S., trends abroad remain stronger, especially in China, according to Truist Securities, in a note released Friday.

"International momentum remains robust," wrote Truist analyst Joseph Civello, noting that most of the company's new stores are being opened in China. "Despite broader macro pressures facing Chinese consumers, [Lululemon's] underlying momentum in the region remains consistent/robust," he added. Truist maintained its buy rating for Lululemon but lowered its price target to $380 from $460.

Related: Lululemon calls out weak brand awareness, as sluggish forecast sinks shares

During the conference call to discuss the results, Lululemon Chief Executive Calvin McDonald said the company needs to drive brand awareness, which he described as a "significant opportunity" for the company globally, particularly in the U.S.

Truist, however, still identified highlights in Lululemon's fourth-quarter numbers. "While we are disappointed in the tepid outlook, we would highlight that units per transaction and average order value are both higher & markdowns were lower [year-over-year] in 4Q and are expected to remain flat this year, which all suggest underlying brand equity remains solid," wrote Civello.

Truist also cited Google Trends data that indicated search interest in Lululemon has recently improved. "We see opportunities for trends to accelerate as new products and marketing campaigns launch," Civello wrote.

Related: Analysts are cutting first-quarter forecasts more than normal. They still expect growth.

Other analysts agreed. Stifel reaffirmed its buy rating for the company in a note released Thursday, but lowered its price target to $424 from $438.

"New product introductions including the Glow Up, Daydrift, and BeCalm are reportedly resonating with guests following product missteps in 2024," wrote Stifel analyst Jim Duffy. Stifel also pointed to Lululemon's track record for outperformance and tangible near-term drivers, such as an improved product lineup and China.

"We see Lululemon uniquely positioned at the intersection of secular trends and believe international growth contribution is under-appreciated in shares," he wrote. "Building on standout performance and market share gains, we expect LULU to sustain strong growth by both adding additional new consumers to the franchise and continuing expanded uptake of the offering to increase revenue per customer."

"Newness" was again a key theme of Lululemon's earnings conference call, echoing a priority that has been cited by other apparel giants such as Nike Inc. (NKE). McDonald said that consumers are spending less due to increased concerns about inflation and the economy, which is manifesting itself into slower traffic across the industry in the first quarter.

"However, we see guests who visit us responding to the newness and innovations we've brought into our assortment," he said, according to a FactSet transcript. "We believe this is a positive indication."

Related: Lululemon's diagnosis of its current woes? Not enough 'newness.'

"The strong customer response to newness gives us confidence that [Lululemon] can improve [North America] sales growth when traffic stabilizes," BofA Securities analyst Lorraine Hutchinson wrote in a note released Friday. "If this consumer soft patch proves to be short-lived, we expect better product and activations to drive sales upside."

BofA reiterated its buy rating for Lululemon, but lowered its price target to $400 from $480.

Lululemon shares have fallen 24.7% in the last 12 months, compared with the S&P 500 index's SPX gain of 7.7%.

-James Rogers

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: News Highlights: Top Global Markets News of the Day - Friday at 11 AM ET
data.description:
Consumers' Mood Sours in March With Gloomier Economic Outlook

The University of Michigan survey's poorest result since 2022 reflects economic-policy anxiety.

Inflation Is Slightly Hotter Than Expected. This Is Even Before Tariffs.

The Fed's preferred inflation gauge overall rose 2.5% in February, meeting expectations. Core inflation rose 0.4% month over month, more than expected.

Wall Street Braces for Higher Metals Prices as Tariff War Intensifies

Both base and precious metals are among the top-performing assets as the end to the first quarter nears, and the momentum is expected to continue as long as the trade war lasts.

Canada GDP Stalls After Strong Growth in January

Canada's economy showed signs of stalling after notching the strongest growth in months at the start of the year, a possible early sign that an erosion in household and business confidence thanks to tariff threats is beginning to drag on growth.

Europe's Industrial Outlook Buoyed by Defense Drive

Plans to increase defense spending led industry leaders to take a brighter view of their prospects, however service providers and retailers grew more gloomy.

Germany's Unemployment Ticks Higher as Layoffs Persist

The jobless rate rose in March, with accelerated announcements of layoffs at major companies threatening a further uptick in unemployment ahead.

Cool Inflation in France, Spain Hints at Fresh Cuts Ahead

Low inflation in major European economies this month points to a cooling in price rises and tees up fresh cuts to interest rates.

Xi's Message in Rare Meeting With Global CEOs: Defend Trade

China's leader told foreign executives visiting Beijing that globalization is an "unstoppable historical trend."

U.S. Stocks Slip Amid Tariff Concerns

Stocks traded lower while gold and bond prices climbed, signaling market participants are pushing into safer assets.

Trump Ushers In 'New High Water Mark' for Deregulation

The president's tariffs are clouding the outlook for plans to cut rules and Elon Musk's efficiency efforts.

data.description:
China's Antitrust Regulator to Review $23 Billion Panama Ports Deal

A consortium of investors led by BlackRock agreed in early March to buy majority stakes in ports on either end of the Panama Canal from Hong Kong-based CK Hutchison.

Demand for private-equity workers has remained strong through a yearslong slump in the industry's fortunes, but 2025 will be a make-or-break year for firms under pressure to attract the best talent, recruiters say.

BBVA Adjusts Sabadell Offer Further to Reflect Final Dividends

The Spanish lender will now offer one BBVA share and 0.70 euros in cash for every 5.3456 Sabadell ordinary shares.

Banco BPM to Press Ahead With Anima Bid

Banco BPM said it is sticking to its offer for the asset manager, even after the ECB issued a negative view on the application of lighter capital rules to the deal.

Financial Services Roundup: Market Talk

Find insight on NatWest, Banco BPM and more in the latest Market Talks covering financial services.

Fed's Musalem worries that the inflation caused by Trump's tariffs won't be so 'transitory'

Interest-rate hikes may become necessary if upward price movement persists, the St. Louis Fed president says.

Bank of Mexico Makes Second Straight Half-Point Rate Cut

The Bank of Mexico lowered its benchmark interest rate by half of a percentage point for the second meeting in a row as inflation eased and the economy continued to slow, and said further cuts of that size are possible.

Jefferies Results Suggest Wall Street Down but Hardly Out

A surprise revenue drop signals caution on dealmaking under Trump.

What We Lose When the World Goes All-In on Index Investing

Carol Geremia, president of MFS, frets that investors care more about beating indexes and benchmarks and less about finding great long-term investments in companies.

AlbaCore Capital Raises Nearly $2 Billion for Direct Lending Fund

The firm will use the capital to back sectors such as business services and software across Europe.

data.title: Canada Budget Deficit Widens Sharply in January From Year Ago
data.description:
By Paul Vieira

OTTAWA--Canada's budget deficit in January widened significantly from a year ago, the country's Department of Finance said Friday.

The department's Fiscal Monitor publication said the budget deficit in January came in at 5.13 billion Canadian dollars, or the equivalent of $3.59 billion. The January, 2025 shortfall was more than double the C$2.06 billion deficit recorded in the same year-ago month.

With only two months of data remaining in the fiscal 2024-25 year, Canada's budget deficit is presently C$26.85 billion, or wider than the C$25.67 billion recorded in the same year-ago period.

Write to Paul Vieira at paul.vieira@wsj.com

data.title: National Bank Says Expanding Canadian Tire and RBC's Triangle Rewards Program is a 'Sensible Approach'
data.description:
National Bank of Canada said it expects more partnerships over the medium term after Royal Bank of Canada and retailer Canadian Tire (CTC-A.TO) signed a long-term strategic loyalty partnership between their Avion Rewards and Triangle Rewards loyalty programs.

National Bank believes that adding external partnerships to Triangle Rewards is a "prudent approach to expand its member base, accelerate the issuance of eCTM, as well as leverage more first-party data insights to leverage the flywheel effect and increase retail sales in a capital-light model." The bank expects more partnerships over the medium term as CTC has 100% ownership of CTFS and full control over the decision for potential partnerships.

National Bank of Canada maintained Canadian Tire's rating and target of sector perform and $174, respectively, given soft consumer demand and uneven operating performance, even as it sees some positive signs emerging related to sales performance. Analyst Vishal Shreedhar commented: "We note that CTC's True North strategy is expected to accelerate retail growth and loyalty expansion."

data.title: Canada Economics Brief: US President Donald Trump On Truth Social Confirms He Has Spoken With Canada PM Mark Carney
Latam FX, stocks eye weekly losses as Trump's tariffs hit risk-taking
data.description:
Key points:
  • Latam stocks down 2%, FX off 0.6%
  • Mexican peso hits over three-week low
  • Brazil real set for first weekly loss in four

Most Latin American currencies and stocks were set for weekly losses on Friday, as risk-sentiment remained weak after U.S. President Donald Trump's latest volley of tariffs, while markets also assessed an inflation reading out of the United States.

Mexico's peso hit its lowest level in over three weeks, with Friday's decline of 0.5% setting the peso for its fourth straight day of declines.

Brazil's real weakened 0.4%, on pace for its first weekly decline in four. Investors in Latin America's biggest economy would also be watching official data which showed Brazil's jobless rate rose in the quarter through February, which matched economists' expectations.

Risk sentiment remained shaky all through this week after Trump imposed a 25% tariff on imported cars and light trucks starting next week.

The focus is now on U.S. reciprocal tariffs due to be announced on April 2. Trump indicated that the measures may not be the like-for-like levies he has been vowing to impose.

Amidst the tariff worries, Latin American currencies and stocks remained under the pump, with MSCI's index tracking the region's currencies (.MILA00000CUS) set for its second straight week of declines.

Latin American equities (.MILA00000PUS) dipped 2% to hit a two-week low, on track for a near 2% drop for the week.

Brazil's Bovespa 1.2% dropped in a broad-based selloff, while the Argentinian benchmark slid 1.5%.

Investors in Argentina would be on the lookout for more details on a deal between the embattled nation and the International Monetary Fund (IMF), after Economy Minister Luis Caputo said on Thursday that the country is targeting a $20 billion deal.

Adding another layer of uncertainty to global trade, data in the United States showed underlying price pressures increasing by the most in 13 months in February.

"Such resilience in core inflation, persistently above the Federal Reserve's target, suggests expectations for a shift in monetary policy may need to be recalibrated, potentially affecting the timing of interest rate adjustments," said Dan Siluk, head of global short duration & liquidity and portfolio manager at Janus Henderson.

Traders see about 70-basis-points of interest rate cuts by Fed in 2025, implying two 25-bps cuts and an 80% of a third such reduction, according to LSEG data.

Despite some weakness towards the latter half of March, Latin American stocks and currencies are set for solid monthly gains, aided by a global rotation out of U.S. equities and broadly softer greenback.

Back in Asia, the Stock Exchange of Thailand suspended all trading activities for the afternoon session after a strong earthquake struck neighboring Myanmar, the tremors of which were felt in Thailand's capital, Bangkok.

The exchange will begin trading on Monday, March 31.

HIGHLIGHTS

** Chile copper miner Codelco swings to profit in 2024

** Ukraine says mineral deal not final; summary shows US demands more income

** Mexico announces second quarter auctions for government debt

NEW YORK, NY / ACCESS Newswire / March 28, 2025 / If you suffered a loss on your AppLovin Corporation investment and want to learn about a potential recovery under the federal securities laws, follow the link below for more information:

or contact Joseph E. Levi, Esq. via email at jlevi@levikorsinsky.com or call (212) 363-7500 to speak to our team of experienced shareholder advocates.

THE LAWSUIT: A class action securities lawsuit was filed against AppLovin Corporation that seeks to recover losses of shareholders who were adversely affected by alleged securities fraud between May 10, 2023 and February 25, 2025.

CASE DETAILS: According to the complaint, defendants provided investors with material information concerning AppLovin's financial growth and stability. Defendants' statements included, among other things, confidence in AppLovin's launch of its AXON 2.0 digital ad platform and using "cutting-edge AI technologies" to more efficiently match advertisements to mobile games, in addition to expanding into web-based marketing and e-commerce. Moreover, defendants publicly reported impressive financial results, outlooks, and guidance to investors, all while using dishonest advertising practices.

The truth emerged on February 26, 2025, when analyst research reports emerged stating that AppLovin was reverse engineering and exploiting advertising data from Meta Platforms. The reports further alleged AppLovin was utilizing manipulative practices to artificially inflate their own ad click-through and app download rates, such as by having ads click on themselves or utilizing design gimmicks to trigger forced shadow downloads, erroneously inflating installation numbers and, in turn, its profit figures.

Following this news, the price of AppLovin's stock declined from $377.06 per share on February 25, 2025 to $331.00 per share on February 26, 2025.
